title: Quick check: host isolation — what it does, what it breaks

questions.prompt: When you isolate a host via Huntress, what does the agent actually do?
choices: Reconfigures the endpoint's network to block almost all traffic, except a documented allow-list.

choices.label: Powers off the endpoint remotely.
choices.rationale: The endpoint stays powered on.

choices.label: Locks the user out of their account.
choices.rationale: The user can still log in locally. Isolation is network, not account.

choices.label: Encrypts the disk so files can't be exfiltrated.
choices.rationale: Not what isolation does. The network is cut; disk and local files are untouched.

questions.explanation: (Huntress back end, sometimes RMM, sometimes the customer's DC). The endpoint stays powered on; the user can log in locally. Network cut with allow-list; everything else on the endpoint keeps working.

questions.prompt: Does Huntress isolation work outside the customer's corporate network — at home, on hotel WiFi, on cellular?
choices: Yes. The agent enforces the policy.

choices.label: No. Isolation only works on the corporate network where the firewall enforces it.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the mechanism. The agent enforces, not the customer's firewall. That's the differentiating point.

choices.label: Only on the corporate network and on the customer's VPN.
choices.rationale: The agent enforces from anywhere it can reach Huntress.

choices.label: Only on Windows; macOS and Linux endpoints can't be isolated remotely.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. Huntress documents isolation for Windows and macOS both.

questions.explanation: As long as the agent can reach Huntress, isolation applies wherever the endpoint is. Agent-enforced containment travels with the endpoint. That's why it's the SOC's tool.

questions.prompt: A user on an isolated endpoint typically experiences which combination?
choices: Browser shows "no internet"; email can't connect;

choices.label: The endpoint shuts down within 30 seconds.
choices.rationale: The endpoint stays powered on.

choices.label: All local files become read-only.
choices.rationale: Local files are unaffected.

choices.label: The user sees a Huntress logo on their desktop and nothing else.
choices.rationale: Not the experience the lesson describes.

questions.explanation: File shares unavailable; Teams/Zoom/Slack disconnect; local files still accessible; the endpoint is otherwise functional. Network cut, local works. The user thinks their computer is broken; the explanation is what changes that.

questions.prompt: Isolating a host shuts down the endpoint and locks the user out of their account.
questions.explanation: The endpoint stays powered on, the user can log in locally, and local applications work. The network is severed; the rest is fine.

questions.prompt: Put the four moves of the plain-language isolation explanation to a customer in the right order.
items: Name the trigger — "activity that needs immediate containment."
Name the action — "we've put it into a protected isolation state."
Name the limits — "you can still log in locally and use what's on the machine, but it can't reach the network."
Name the next step — "we're working on it; I'll call you when the machine is ready to come out of isolation."
questions.explanation: Trigger, action, limits, next step. Patience scales with knowing what comes next.

questions.prompt: You just isolated WS-EXAMPLE-FINANCE-08 per a High Incident Report. The user, Sarah, calls panicking: "My computer is broken. I have a quarterly report due in two hours." What do you tell Sarah?
choices: Use the four-move explanation (trigger, action, limits, next step).

choices.label: "We've blocked your machine because of a security issue. You need to wait."
choices.rationale: "Blocked" frames the action as MSP damage; tells Sarah nothing about scope or timing.

choices.label: "Your computer was compromised. We had to take it offline. Don't touch anything."
choices.rationale: Overclaim. "Compromised" without qualification scares Sarah; "don't touch anything" gives her nothing to do for an hour.

questions.explanation: Then answer her specific concern — if the report is local she can work on it; if it's on a network share she'll need to wait. Four moves plus the specific answer for her specific concern. Honest about the timeline, honest about what still works.

questions.prompt: Sarah asks: "But the report is on a network share. Can you let me just connect to that one share? Just for the report?" What do you tell her?

choices.label: "Yes, I'll allow that one share through the isolation."
choices.rationale: Isolation policy is binary at the agent level; the allow-list isn't a per-customer-request surface. Opening it defeats the containment.

choices: "I can't open the network up while the machine is isolated;

choices.label: "I'm sorry, you'll have to wait until we release it."
choices.rationale: Defensible but incomplete. Says no without helping Sarah figure out how to meet her deadline. The relationship piece is in offering alternatives.

questions.explanation: That's the whole point. What I can do is get this resolved as fast as possible. While you wait — can you work from another machine, your phone, a tablet? If the file is in SharePoint or OneDrive you can access it anywhere; if it's only on the share I can have a colleague pull a copy to a clean location." Honest about the can't, useful about the can, creative about workarounds without weakening isolation.

questions.prompt: "Isolation is reversible without consequence." Why does the lesson reject this?

choices.label: Un-isolation always succeeds; the consequence is brief downtime, nothing more.
choices.rationale: Inverts the warning. The lesson is explicit that premature un-isolation re-exposes the network.

choices.label: Un-isolation requires a senior's approval every time.
choices.rationale: The recovery checklist governs the decision; not every un-isolation needs senior approval.

choices.label: Un-isolation invalidates the customer's Huntress licence for 24 hours.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the commercial model.

questions.explanation: Un-isolating with the threat unresolved puts the same problem back on the network. Reversibility doesn't equal safety. The recovery checklist (next lesson) is what makes un-isolation safe.

questions.prompt: Which is part of the typical isolation allow-list?

choices.label: Microsoft 365 mail and SharePoint, so the user can keep working.
choices.rationale: M365 is on the blocked side. Allowing it would defeat the containment.

choices.label: The user's bookmarked websites.
choices.rationale: Not on the allow-list; isolation cuts internet access.

choices.label: Everything except a deny-list of known-bad domains.
choices.rationale: Inverts the model. Isolation is default-deny with an allow-list.

questions.explanation: (so the agent can keep reporting and you can un-isolate later), plus a small set of management exceptions the runbook may specify (RMM, sometimes the customer's DC). Huntress back end + management exceptions per the runbook. Everything else is cut.

questions.prompt: A user reports "Outlook is broken on my machine but Teams works on my phone." The affected workstation has an active Huntress Incident Report. What's the likely explanation?
choices: Single-machine network failure with the phone unaffected is isolation-shaped.

choices.label: Outlook is corrupted on the workstation; reinstall it.
choices.rationale: Possible in general, but with an active Incident Report on the host, isolation is the more parsimonious explanation. Check that first.

choices.label: Microsoft 365 is having a regional outage.
choices.rationale: A regional outage would affect Teams on the phone too. Phone working rules this out.

choices.label: The user's password expired.
choices.rationale: Wouldn't match the single-machine pattern; the phone uses the same account.

questions.explanation: The workstation is probably isolated. Single-machine network failure + active Incident Report = check the portal for isolation before troubleshooting Outlook.
